metadata.title: Diagnose a Model's Causal Claims with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to catch the causal sleight-of-hand when a model tells me X causes Y. Take a paragraph of model output I paste in and diagnose its causal claims. Concretely: (1) extract each implied cause-effect assertion and label it as correlational, causal, or ambiguous, (2) for every causal one, name the confounder or selection effect that could explain it away, and (3) tell me which single claim, if wrong, most damages the overall conclusion.

props.command: I want to catch the causal sleight-of-hand when a model tells me X causes Y. Take a paragraph of model output I paste in and diagnose its causal claims. Concretely: (1) extract each implied cause-effect assertion and label it as correlational, causal, or ambiguous, (2) for every causal one, name the confounder or selection effect that could explain it away, and (3) tell me which single claim, if wrong, most damages the overall conclusion.
props.segment: Diagnose a Model's Causal Claims with

beats.narration: A churn model confidently predicts — but is it measuring association or causation? Claude runs a do-operator diagnosis and finds the overreach.
P of Y given X is what the model learned. P of Y given do of X is what you need to intervene. Most features give you the first — not the second.
We ask Claude to diagnose each feature: does the observational training give us leverage over the causal claim?

props.command:
claude "For this churn model [feature list],
  for each feature write:
    P(churn|feature=high) — observational
    P(churn|do(feature=high)) — causal
    verdict: safe / causal overreach / confound"

beats.narration: Claude outputs the diagnosis table. Login frequency and last-login days are flagged as potential confounds — plan type likely drives both. NPS is flagged as causal overreach.

props.code:
# causal_diagnosis.py — Claude Code output
features = [
  # (name, P_obs, verdict)
  ('login_freq',    0.42, 'potential confound'),
  ('support_tickets',0.58,'safe'),
  ('plan_price',    0.31, 'causal overreach'),
  ('last_login_days',0.71,'potential confound'),
  ('nps_score',     0.65, 'causal overreach'),
]
for name, p_obs, verdict in features:
    flag = '⚠' if verdict != 'safe' else '✓'
    print(f'{flag} {name}: P(churn|obs)={p_obs}  → {verdict}')

beats.narration: The diagnosis table. Five features, three columns: observational probability, causal claim status, and verdict. Two safe, two confounds, one overreach.
We ask Claude to add a fourth column: what intervention would actually test the causal claim for each feature?

props.command:
claude "Add column 4: for each feature,
  what intervention would test
  the causal claim?
  Be specific and plausible."

beats.narration: The intervention column. Support tickets: randomize contact rate. Plan price: run a price experiment. NPS: send a satisfaction survey intervention. Each one is a testable do-operation.
Most ML features are observational proxies. The do-operator exposes which features would survive an intervention — and which would collapse the moment you tried to act on them.
Next: enumerate all backdoor paths in a pricing DAG and find the adjustment set that blocks them all.
Like and subscribe for more causal reasoning with Claude.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Diagnose a Model's Causal Claims with Claude] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
